data.title: The Fed Cuts, But Confidence Wavers: Our Outlook
data.description:
The Federal Open Market Committee (FOMC) delivered a widely expected 25 basis point rate cut at its October meeting, marking the second consecutive reduction and bringing the target range to 3.75-4.00%.

However, expectations for further easing quickly faded after Chairman Jerome Powell cautioned that a December rate cut was “not a foregone conclusion – far from it.” Before the meeting, markets had effectively priced another cut in December as inevitable, but odds have since dropped to near even, injecting fresh uncertainty into the final FOMC meeting of 2025.

For investors, this shifting outlook reinforces the importance of maintaining flexibility in fixed income positioning. Duration exposure has become more sensitive to data releases and Fed communications, making active management essential in the weeks ahead.

The Fed continues to navigate a challenging backdrop: a steadily cooling labor market alongside inflation that remains stubbornly above its long-run target. Progress on price stability has stalled in recent quarters as core goods inflation rises, influenced by new trade tariffs, a weaker US dollar, and improving global growth.

Still, most FOMC members appear increasingly concerned about labor market softness. Job openings have declined steadily, unemployment hovers near 4.3%, and private sector hiring outside of healthcare and education has been negative for two straight quarters. Moderating wage growth may help restrain services inflation and support the case for further policy easing.

For investors, this mixed data underscores the value of diversifying sources of income, which could include short-duration bonds, multi-sector credit, and selective alternatives that can perform across varying rate environments.

The recent government shutdown – the longest on record – has complicated the Fed’s assessment by limiting access to official data. Private indicators show a continued but gradual slowdown in employment, with notable headlines on layoffs at major companies.

Given still loose financial conditions, the case for additional easing remains ambiguous. There is a meaningful chance the Fed pauses and leaves rates unchanged in December.

2026 Thoughts

Looking beyond year-end, markets anticipate the policy rate could move toward 3.0% by the end of 2026, while the Fed’s own projections remain slightly more cautious, with a median forecast around 3.4%.

A terminal rate in the low 3% range appears achievable if labor markets continue to weaken. One emerging source of uncertainty is the leadership transition expected when Chair Powell’s term expires in mid-2026, as political pressure builds for a more dovish successor.

The MOVE Index, which tracks implied volatility in US rates markets, now sits near its three-year low. Investors should consider maintaining exposure to the tails of the distribution, as complacency may prove costly in a year shaped by policy uncertainty and shifting macro dynamics.

We can say with conviction entering 2026 that volatility – not just in interest rates but across all asset classes – will define the year ahead. Despite historically low implied volatility across rates, FX, credit, and equities, the potential for sharp dislocations is rising.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Tradeweb Exchange-Traded Funds Update - October 2025
data.description:
The following data is derived from trading activity on the Tradeweb Markets institutional European- and U.S.-listed ETF platforms.

European-Listed ETFs

Total traded volume

Trading activity on the Tradeweb European ETF marketplace amounted to EUR 72.8 billion in October. The percentage of transactions and notional volume completed via Tradeweb’s Automated Intelligent Execution (AiEX) tool registered at 94% and 30%, respectively.

Adam Gould, Global Head of Equities at Tradeweb, said, “Our European ETF platform delivered another strong performance in October, with the combination of broad client-base growth, increased ETF adoption and our automated rules-based RFQ trading protocol driving deeper liquidity and greater efficiency for the market.”

Volume breakdown

In October, equities remained the most actively traded ETF asset class, comprising 63% of the overall platform flow. In commodity-based funds, ‘sells’ exceeded ‘buys’ by 42 percentage points, whereas equity- and fixed income-based products saw net buying by 18 and 8 percentage points, respectively.

North America Equities reclaimed its spot as the most heavily traded ETF category during the month, generating over EUR 15 billion in monthly volume, after Global Equities briefly topped the table in September.

Top ten by traded notional volume

In October, the two most actively traded ETFs were commodity-based, with the remainder consisting of equity- and fixed income-based funds. The Invesco Physical Gold ETC and the iShares Physical Gold ETC, which both offer exposure to the performance of the gold spot price, placed first and second, while the iShares Core S&P 500 UCITS ETF came in third.

U.S.-Listed ETFs

Total traded volume

Total consolidated U.S. ETF notional value traded in October reached USD 91.3 billion.

Adam Gould, Global Head of Equities at Tradeweb, said, “October was the third best-performing month on record for our U.S. institutional ETF platform, with volumes increasing by 59.6% year-over-year, as clients increasingly rely on streamlined, scalable execution. Meanwhile, the percentage of transactions executed via AiEX reached an all-time high of 51%.”

Volume breakdown

As a percentage of total notional value, equities accounted for 54% and fixed income for 39%, with the remainder comprising commodity and specialty ETFs.

Top ten by traded notional volume

In a reversal of September’s trend, seven of the top ten ETFs by traded notional volume in October were fixed income-based, with the remainder being equity-based.

The iShares US Treasury Bond ETF, which last appeared in the top three in March 2024, placed first. Meanwhile, the JP Morgan BetaBuilders Europe ETF and Vanguard 500 Index Fund ETF came in second and third place, respectively.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Chinese Data Shows Momentum Fading Into The Final Stretch Of The Year
data.description:
By Lynn Song, Chief Economist, Greater China

Investment continues to disappoint amid downbeat forecasts as corporates stay sidelined

Chinese fixed asset investment slumped further to -1.7% year-over-year, year-to-date, marking the lowest level since June 2020. Once again, the data is falling well short of already downbeat forecasts.

By category, we saw infrastructure investment fall into contraction territory at -0.1% YoY ytd, contracting for the first time since 2020. Manufacturing investment, which had been a solid outperformer for several years, slumped to just 2.7% YoY ytd. Private sector investment fell to -4.5% YoY ytd, while even government investment slowed to 0.1% YoY ytd and looks likely to tip into contraction territory next month.

The only silver linings were seen in China's recent growth areas, namely the auto (17.5%) and rail, ships, and aeroplanes (20.1%) categories.

While the magnitude of the declines was somewhat surprising, the continued weakness of investment itself was not. Anti-involution policies targeting excessive price competition and the shift toward reducing redundant investments could be adding to the downward pressure. Yesterday's poor credit data showed new RMB loans actually contracted by RMB 20.1bn in October. While October tends to be a seasonally weak month due to the Golden Week holidays, the lack of borrowing demand has been quite clear year-to-date, with new RMB loans down 7.4% year-to-date. All this suggests real interest rates remain too high to attract prospective borrowers.

FAI is slowing across the board

Property pressures may persist absent new support

October's 70 city property prices continued to show downward pressure. New home prices fell by -0.45% month-on-month, the steepest decline since October 2024. Used home prices fell by -0.66% MoM, the steepest decline since September 2024. After flatlining in the first quarter, prices have fallen at a faster pace. From the peak, new home prices are now down -11.8%, while used home prices are down -20.3%. Of the 70 cities, 45 have seen secondary market prices decline between 20% and 30% from their peak, while 3 have experienced price drops of more than 30% from the peak.

In the primary market, 6 of 70 cities saw prices stabilise or increase, the lowest proportion since September 2024. In the secondary market, we saw a second consecutive month without a single city seeing stable or rising price levels.

With falling prices and still elevated inventory levels, it is no surprise to see that property investment remains one of the biggest drags on the economy, with property investment now down -14.7% YoY ytd.

The recent downturn, with accelerating price declines, has yet to be adequately addressed. More policy support is needed to prevent the massive destruction of household wealth, which would significantly hinder efforts to transition toward a consumption-driven growth model.

Secondary market prices have mostly fallen 20% or more from the peak

Retail sales moderated by less than expected

October's retail sales data slowed to 2.9% year-on-year, down only 0.1pp from September's reading. That’s a little stronger than market expectations. Year-to-date, retail sales are up 4.3% YoY and should still be able to close 2025 stronger than last year's 3.5% growth. But the loss of momentum in the second half of the year nonetheless remains a little disappointing given the stated importance of domestic demand.

The major outperformer in October was seen in gold and jewellery sales, which rose to 37.6% YoY, the highest level since April 2023, as retail buying picked up with gold reaching its all-time high in October.

As we discussed in the past few months of reports, the impact of the trade-in policy continues to fade. Key beneficiary categories, including household appliances, communication appliances, and furniture, have been on a downward trend. In particular, we saw household appliance sales plunge -14.6% YoY in October, as similar promotions in 2024 create an unfavourable base effect. This pattern has previously been seen in auto sales, one of the earliest beneficiaries of the trade-in policy. A similar drag will likely emerge for communication appliances early next year.

While the policy has been successful in frontloading consumption this year, a new policy direction will likely be needed to support consumption next year.

Fading trade-in policy impact suggests a new direction for consumption support may be needed

Industrial production slowdown to be monitored closely

October value added of industry growth slowed to 4.9% YoY, down from 6.5%. This came in below expectations for a smaller decline, marking the lowest growth since August 2024. The year-to-date growth edged down 0.1pp to 6.1% YoY as a result.

By industry, we continue to see the same themes outperform. Auto manufacturing (16.8%) and rail, ships, and aeroplane manufacturing (15.2%) remain clear outperformers. We’re still seeing strong growth in semiconductor (17.7%), industrial robot (17.9%), and service robot (12.8%) production. Advanced manufacturing was identified as one of the top priorities for China in the next Five-Year Plan and is expected to continue experiencing strong growth and investment.

Industrial production has been a key driver of growth, with manufacturing meeting external demand this year. Soft industrial production confirmed the weakness we saw earlier in the month with the steeper-than-expected downturn of the purchasing managers' index. While one month of soft data does not necessarily indicate a trend, the next few months appear to have a less supportive base effect. The deceleration of industrial production should be watched closely along with exports. Signs that external demand and manufacturing are slowing will represent significant risks to the overall outlook, given the softness of domestic demand.

Industrial production slowed in October

Slowdown suggests that policy support direction won't change

Following the continued softness of the October data and an uptick in inflation, which could reduce the tailwind from the GDP deflator, risks to our 2025 GDP growth forecast of 5.0% appear somewhat balanced to the downside heading into the final stretch of the year.

There’s reduced urgency for new policy support. Stronger-than-expected growth in the first three quarters of the year is likely keeping the economy on track to meet its 2025 growth target without requiring much additional intervention. With ambitious targets set by Premier Li for 2030, targeting a GDP of RMB 170tn, along with President Xi's aim from 2020 to double growth by 2035, it will be a marathon, not a sprint.

As such, the recent lull in new stimulus is likely only a pause rather than a reversal of direction. Policymakers may opt to conserve ammunition for next year's growth targets. We still see room for further monetary easing next year, and fiscal support for consumption and strategic industries will likely continue.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more

Original Post

data.title: Pfizer Strengthens Pipeline With $10B Metsera Deal Amid Solid Q3 Results
data.description:
Thesis

Pfizer Inc. (PFE) posted some pretty strong 3Q25 results, although the stock reaction was a bit dull. We saw a Non-GAAP EPS of $0.87, which beat estimates by about $0.23, and revenue of $16.65 billion, also beating forecasts by $150 million despite an overall 5.9% year-over-year decline. Management also reaffirmed its 2025 revenue outlook of $61 to $64 billion and actually raised EPS guidance to $3.00 to $3.15. They're hoping for stronger execution and cost discipline going forward. Pfizer also said it remains on track to achieve $7.2 billion in net cost savings by 2027, which should help out with margin expansion. The announcement initially sent shares up more than 2% in premarket trading.

Elsewhere, I thought the news of earnings was a bit overshadowed by the public bidding war with Novo Nordisk (NVO) over Metsera Inc. (MTSR). The last blow was a knockout and saw Pfizer match Novo's $10 billion call to enter into an amended merger agreement with Metsera, and now, as of today, Metsera shareholders have voted in favour of the merger. Pfizer stock is up roughly 1.5% as a result. They're now set to acquire the company for $86.25 per share. I think the merger should be good for Pfizer's portfolio in the long run, as I'll explain. I say long run here, as it could be a while before Metsera's obesity treatment hits the market. The good news is that the stock is cheap, relative to the sector. Shares can be picked up in the mid-$26 range premarket.

3Q25 review

Pfizer’s 3Q25 results were a bit mixed, but fundamentally constructive for investors. Total revenues were $16.7 billion, down about 6% compared to the same period last year. So there's been some continued pullback in COVID-19 product sales even as their non-COVID portfolio grew. Non-COVID operational revenue actually saw a 4% bump. Adjusted non-GAAP diluted EPS for the quarter was $0.87, with reported diluted EPS at $0.62. Management also raised and narrowed full-year 2025 adjusted EPS guidance to $3.00 to $3.15, while keeping their revenue guidance of $61.0 to $64.0 billion intact. The commercial drivers included a robust demand for Eliquis, which was up roughly 22% operationally, and strong execution across newly launched and acquired products.

Headwinds for the company are, of course, the lower Paxlovid and Comirnaty volumes and that one-time $1.35 billion acquired IPR&D charge tied to the in-licensing deal with 3SBio. That payment alone reduced near-term reported results by roughly $0.20 per share. Pfizer also reiterated its multi-year cost-savings roadmap; they still seem on track for approximately $7.2 billion of net savings by the end of 2027. Management also signaled towards more disciplined capital allocation with continued dividends and limited buybacks for 2025.

Merger with Metsera

Metsera shareholders have just today approved the company’s $10 billion sale to Pfizer. The decision follows a small bidding war with Novo, and under the revised merger agreement, Pfizer will acquire Metsera for up to $86.25 per share in cash. Now, this Metsera acquisition marks one of the most consequential biotech deals of 2025, in my opinion, purely due to the fact that it showed the market just how fiercely competitive the weight-loss drug space has become. As you know, Metsera is a U.S.-based clinical-stage biotech specialising in next-generation obesity and metabolic therapies. Its pipeline currently includes both injectable and oral candidates that target some novel metabolic pathways, including nutrient-stimulated hormone (NuSH) mechanisms. The interesting part there is that the mechanism in question is tipped to achieve sustained weight loss with fewer gastrointestinal side effects than current GLP-1 treatments.

So you can understand just why the company was so hotly contested. In fact, one of Metsera’s leading programs, MET-097i, has actually shown double-digit placebo-adjusted weight loss in early trials. It was a potential to rival/complement to existing blockbusters like Novo’s Wegovy, and they were willing to pay up big for the treatment in the $10 billion range. If we take this scientific promise and combine it with the explosive growth of the global obesity market, which we’ve seen so far, it’s expected to exceed $100 billion annually by the early 2030s if all goes well. It’s a figure that made Metsera an attractive target and sparked a pretty rare public bidding war between Pfizer and Novo.

For Pfizer, the deal is now set to be a massive pivot for them and, honestly, a much-needed growth catalyst. After the steep decline we’ve been seeing in COVID-19 revenues and setbacks to its other internal obesity drug programs, Metsera is giving Pfizer a fresh entry point here. The acquisition should give Pfizer a credible, diversified pipeline in metabolic disease. It was an area it once dominated, but has recently lagged behind in. Elsewhere, the company’s willingness to match the final $10 billion offer shows me they have the willingness for a long-term commitment to rebuilding its innovation pipeline. We’re obviously not going to see the Metsera treatment in action for a while, but it gives them a big boost down the line when it comes to competing head-on with Novo and Eli Lilly.

Under the newly revised terms, Metsera shareholders will receive $65.60 per share upfront, plus up to $20.65 per share in contingent value rights tied to any future milestones. The transaction is now set to close following Metsera’s shareholder vote on November 13th. The good news for Pfizer is that it can now start to reassert itself as a major player in obesity therapeutics. What investors now need to follow is whether Metsera’s candidates can deliver on their early promise. Investors should keep a lookout for the Phase 2 results of MET-097i in 2026. They should, in particular, keep an eye on the weight-loss efficacy and safety data. The bullish case for entering the weight loss market would see an FDA fast-track designation later down the line.

Regulatory Risk

The biggest risk here, as far as investors are concerned, lies in clinical execution and regulatory confidence. While the early data for MET-097i is indeed promising, the program, keep in mind, is very early-stage. It means efficacy and safety outcomes in Phase 2 and Phase 3 trials are yet to provide that big confidence boost going into an FDA application. And Pfizer has already paid up big time with $10 billion. Another risk is integration and cost management. We have to remember that Pfizer is already balancing several recent acquisitions alongside that ongoing $7.2 billion cost-savings plan. As part of the acquisition, they'll have to efficiently absorb Metsera’s R&D operations.

Valuation

Pfizer’s valuation appears quite compelling, relative to both the historical figures and the broader sector. The stock over the last period hasn't given investors the returns they would have wanted, with shares actually down about 1% year to date. They hold a forward price to sales ratio of 2.21x and a forward EV-to-sales ratio of 2.97x, so the stock trades at roughly 40% to 45% below sector medians and well under its own five-year averages. These metrics suggest investors are still pricing in the company’s expected continued post-COVID revenue decline and pipeline execution risks. But let's not forget we should see some potential upside from cost savings and the push for more portfolio diversification.

At these levels, I think Pfizer can offer a rare mix of deep value and high income. There’s also a 7% dividend yield that’s over four times the sector average. On the balance sheet, flexibility looks good, and so does investment-grade credit. The company holds nearly $15 billion in cash against $62 billion in total debt, giving it manageable net leverage of around 1.3x EBITDA, well within investment-grade thresholds. They also hold an A-range credit rating from major agencies. So even with the $10 billion Metsera purchase, Pfizer’s liquidity position and operating cash flows, projected at over $20 billion annually, should provide that flexibility to fund dividends in the foreseeable future.

Looking ahead

So, just to conclude, investors should watch Pfizer’s Metsera obesity pipeline progress, albeit being a long way out. Positive news on this front should add stock momentum through 2026. Especially Phase 2/3 trial results and any FDA regulatory updates to come. Key metrics would also include the tipped cost-savings execution and how the company manages the integration of recent acquisitions. At $26, I think the stock is a buy; investors are getting in early on a long road to portfolio recovery.

data.title: Sherwin-Williams: Likely Matte Future For Shareholders
data.description:
Intro

In summer, I wrote my first analysis about Sherwin-Williams (SHW), giving the stock a "Strong Sell" rating. SHW today trades to the dollar on the same level as back then. In between, it has been swinging around the current price point.

Today, I will evaluate the recent Q3 results and give a short update. However, my strong sell rating remains in place, as I continue to view SHW stock’s valuation to be "off the wall" and highly optimistic, as I had put it in my first article.

Coated in high expectations

Sherwin-Williams reported Q3 2025 sales of $6.36B (+3.2% YoY), surprisingly beating estimates. Adjusted EPS of $3.59 (+6.5%) topped consensus by 4%. Gross margins expanded for the 12th straight quarter, aided by lower input costs and a favorable mix. The Paint Stores Group led with +3.6% same-store sales, capturing share from professional contractors despite an overall sluggish macro backdrop.

That was the good news.

Industrial and consumer channels lagged, reflecting broader demand weakness. The environment remains "softer for longer," with CEO Heidi Petz warning of persistent headwinds into 2026. I think this statement is something we will hear and read from more and more CEOs over the next months; that’s why I am skeptical about short-term improvements. I am thankful for this openness. Consumer spending is tepid, especially in DIY and big-ticket home projects, while industrial end-markets face inventory destocking and uncertainty. Latin America offers growth via the new Suvinil acquisition, but regional softness tempers optimism, too.

For a cyclical company (and stock) like SHW, this assessment of the operating environment should be taken seriously, even though the stock seems to be immune to the core message, at least for now.

Cost discipline and restructuring ($40M savings in 2025) bolstered EBITDA margins to 21.4% (+60 bps). Capex remains high for new HQ/R&D facilities but is expected to normalize in 2026, benefitting free cash flow.

Guidance calls for Q4 and full-year 2025 sales to be up low-single digits, with EPS near $11.35. Management leans on self-help – pricing, share gains, efficiency (cost control) – to offset weak demand.

While this sounds consequential, there is a problem. With shares trading at a premium for a company with such an undemanding growth dynamic, the outlook lacks the vibrancy to justify current valuation.

No matter which metric we look at, the multiples are on the upper end of the historical norm. Let’s start with EV / sales.

Even if the last cyclical low of 2x EV / sales will not be reached (this is more a worst-case type of target), nothing speaks against a sharp correction of 25–30% to fall back into the average of the historical range. We‘ve been there just a few years ago. SHW stock dropped from $300 to $200 relatively quickly.

The expectation is the same by looking at the market cap to free cash flow – which is even ignoring net debt of ~$12 billion. SHW likely will not be a 10x PE or FCF stock, but why not 20–25x instead of a lofty 40x?

I don‘t like this metric, but the PE ratio is telling us the same.

With single-digit growth and a shaky business environment, SHW stock has become a high-risk play. Even though sometimes forgotten, valuation is also a risk factor to determine investment success. I would not be surprised, if in a couple of years, investors will be scratching their heads, wondering how SHW stock was able to trade at such multiples.

That said, I view my first assessment from summer as not only confirmed, but my “strong sell“ rating is even firmer than before.

Risks to My Thesis

SHW stock could continue to climb further, supported by its Dow Jones membership and general market strength.

The cyclical downswing could be milder than expected.

SHW is putting a brake on costs, gobbling up smaller competitors, and making use of its vast scale. This could limit the downside due to its strong competitive factors.

Conclusion

SHW stock remains a "Strong Sell" like in my first article. The business environment remains tough, while the stock is fully priced. What is not priced in, is a correction of this cyclical business. The weak guidance sooner or later should be painted into the stock.

data.title: Moat Stocks Climb In A Concentrated Market
data.description:
Markets extended gains in October as earnings strength, AI investment, and Fed easing supported sentiment, helping moat strategies show selective strength.

In October, the S&P 500 marked its sixth straight monthly gain and set fresh record highs, as a strong third quarter earnings season and announcements of continued AI-driven capital spending kept risk appetite intact. Equities were further supported by the Federal Reserve’s second 25-basis point cut of the year and the signaling of an end to quantitative tightening. Hopes for an incremental thaw in U.S.-China trade relations also helped, rounding out a constructive backdrop heading into November. However, market breadth remained narrow during the month. Leadership continued to be concentrated in the typical mega-cap technology names, while small caps faced pressure from the continued government shutdown and resulting gaps in critical economic data releases.

The Morningstar Wide Moat Focus Index (the “Moat Index”) gained 2.66% in October, outpacing the S&P 500, which advanced 2.34%. Strong stock selection was the primary driver of relative outperformance, led by health care and industrials, where several holdings beat sector peers. The outperformance was despite headwinds to equal-weight strategies during the month, illustrated by the equal-weight S&P 500, which finished down nearly a full percentage point.

In contrast, smaller cap companies trailed, as investors continued to favor large-cap technology leaders even after another quarter-point rate cut. The Morningstar US Small-Mid Cap Moat Focus Index (the “SMID Moat Index”) declined 0.60% during the month, falling in between the broad small- and mid-cap benchmarks given the strategy’s mixed size exposure.

Wide Moat Stock Shows Strength as Market Breadth Shrinks

Moat Index Highlights: Semis and Science Lead

In October, the Moat Index finished ahead of the S&P 500, overcoming headwinds faced generally by equal-weighted strategies during the month. Strong stock selection was the primary driver of relative performance, but sector allocations in certain segments, namely overweights in health care and industrials, also proved beneficial during the month.

Teradyne (TER) was the top contributor to the Moat Index in October as shares rose 32% following a strong earnings release. Third quarter sales rose 4%, which was at the high end of guidance, and management called for additional revenue growth in the fourth quarter on accelerating AI demand. Morningstar highlights a stronger position in AI chip testing, including custom accelerators from Broadcom (AVGO) and high bandwidth memory, with rising confidence that Teradyne can qualify as a second testing source for GPUs at Nvidia (NVDA) and AMD (AMD). With AI now the primary growth driver and mobile becoming less central, Morningstar raised its fair value estimate to $140 per share from $115 and reiterated a wide moat view on the firm’s automated test leadership. While the stock looks slightly rich to that estimate after the move, Morningstar expects double-digit chip testing growth over the next two years as AI buildouts add capacity and complexity.

Also within the top contributors this month was Thermo Fisher Scientific Inc. (TMO), a global leader in life science tools and services that span instruments, consumables, logistics, and clinical trial support. Shares gained 17% in October after the company announced the $8.9B acquisition of Clario Holdings, a provider of clinical trial data solutions, with closing targeted for mid-2026. Clario’s endpoint data software is used in roughly 70% of U.S. drug approvals and will expand Thermo Fisher’s clinical trial solutions portfolio. Management expects Clario’s $1.25B revenue base to grow at a high single-digit rate and be immediately accretive to adjusted operating margin. TMO’s wide moat reflects unmatched scale, a one-stop-shop portfolio, and switching costs that come from deep integration with pharma workflows and a large base of recurring consumables and services. Morningstar maintains a $630 per share fair value estimate and a wide moat rating.

Other top contributors within the Moat Index during the month included semiconductor equipment leader Applied Materials Inc. (AMAT), life science tools and diagnostics provider Agilent Technologies Inc. (A), and global package delivery and logistics provider United Parcel Service Inc. (UPS).

Companies detracting the most in October included consumer health company Kenvue Inc. (KVUE), semiconductor leader NXP Semiconductors (NXPI), global food and beverage company Mondelez International Inc. (MDLZ), household products maker Clorox Co. (CLX), and athletic footwear and apparel brand Nike Inc. (NKE).

Moat Index Top Contributors and Detractors - October 2025

Contributors

Company Ticker Sector Avg. Weight (%) Contribution (%)
Teradyne Inc. TER Technology 1.89 0.61
Thermo Fisher Scientific Inc. TMO Health Care 2.67 0.45
Applied Materials Inc. AMAT Technology 3.03 0.42
Agilent Technologies Inc. A Health Care 2.57 0.36
United Parcel Service Inc. UPS Industrials 2.25 0.35

Detractors

Company Ticker Sector Avg. Weight (%) Contribution (%)
Kenvue Inc KVUE Consumer Staples 2.02 -0.23
NXP Semiconductors NXPI Technology 2.54 -0.21
Mondelez International Inc. MDLZ Consumer Staples 2.38 -0.19
Clorox Co. CLX Consumer Staples 2.39 -0.19
Nike Inc. NKE Consumer Discretionary 2.51 -0.19

Source: Morningstar. Past performance is no guarantee of future results. Index performance is not illustrative of fund performance. Not intended as a recommendation to buy or to sell any of the securities mentioned herein.

SMID Moat Index Highlights: Cables and Capsules Climb

The SMID Moat Index performance was muted this month, falling in between the small- and mid-cap benchmarks given its exposure to both size cohorts. The strategy benefited from its overweight allocation in health care, a top performing sector during the month, as well as from its underweight to the financials. However, headwinds to consumer discretionary names acted as an offset.

WESCO International (WCC) was the top contributor to the SMID Moat Index in October as shares rallied 23% on strong quarterly earnings results. Organic sales increased and EPS grew year-over-year despite some margin pressure. Momentum remained strongest in data center solutions, marking a fifth straight quarter with sales up more than 50% and bringing that end market to nearly 20% of WESCO's revenue. WESCO is also benefiting from U.S. infrastructure spending and multiyear projects where it provides value-added services. Morningstar raised its fair value estimate to $240 and maintained WCC’s economic moat. The moat reflects scale, broad supplier and product reach, and service capabilities such as vendor-managed inventory that deepen customer relationships and support a cost advantage.

Ionis Pharmaceuticals Inc. (IONS) was a top contributor for the second consecutive month, rising about 13% in October. Momentum followed strong uptake for their rare disease drug, Tryngolza, which delivered $32M in quarterly sales and grew 68% sequentially, prompting a higher revenue guidance outlook. Management is also planning near-term U.S. filings for two additional products, olezarsen and zilganersen, and Morningstar assigns a 90% approval probability to each with launches expected in 2026. Ionis’ narrow moat is supported by its proprietary antisense platform and layered intellectual property and by progress building independent commercial capabilities. Morningstar maintains a $74 fair value estimate and a positive long-term outlook.

Other top contributors include Agilent Technologies Inc., a life science tools and diagnostics provider, Huntington Ingalls Industries (HII), the largest U.S. military shipbuilder, and Albemarle Corp. (ALB), a leading lithium producer serving electric vehicle supply chains.

Companies detracting the most in October within the SMID Moat Index spanned multiple sectors, but consumer discretionary was the notable stand out. Names included online sports betting firm DraftKings Inc. (DKNG), cruise operator Norwegian Cruise Line Ltd. (NCLH), sleep and respiratory care device maker ResMed Inc. (RMD), confectionery company The Hershey Co. (HSY), and alternative asset manager The Carlyle Group Inc. (CG).

SMID Moat Index Top Contributors and Detractors - October 2025

Contributors

Company Ticker Sector Avg. Weight (%) Contribution (%)

Choose Your Moat Strategy

data.title: Wasatch U.S. Select Fund: 6 Stocks That Showed Strong Revenue And Earnings Growth
data.description:
The following segment was excerpted from the Wasatch U.S. Select Fund Q3 2025 Commentary.

BellRing Brands, Inc.

BellRing Brands, Inc. (BRBR) was the largest detractor from Fund performance during the quarter. BellRing is a consumer-staples company known for its Premier Protein line and other convenient-nutrition products. What’s weighed on the stock most is that one of BellRing’s club channel partners is expanding the floor space of the protein shake category and giving some space to other competitors. We spoke with management after that news and reevaluated the business. We came out of that work with continued conviction in the company. We believe BellRing has maintained its leadership position, and that this floor space expansion reflects the health of the category. Looking ahead, we continue to believe BellRing has headroom to double its business, supported by category growth, sourcing share from incumbent brands, and remaining a leader in its space.

Shift4 Payments, Inc.

Another detractor was Shift4 Payments, Inc. (FOUR). A longtime holding in many of our small-cap funds, Shift4 provides payment processing solutions for hospitality, retail, and e‑commerce businesses. Fundamentals for the company remain solid, as organic revenues grew in the high-teens percentages in the most recent quarter. But the stock has been down in part due to concerns about how a softer macroeconomic environment could affect the company. Additionally, investor sentiment on the company became less favorable after its acquisition of Global Blue, which is a foray into a new customer segment for Shift4. The acquisition also means the company is taking on more debt. Acquisitions can sometimes cause investors angst since they take time to integrate into a company’s normal operations. However, we have confidence in Shift4’s leadership and their ability to execute on Global Blue.

Inspire Medical Systems, Inc.

Inspire Medical Systems, Inc. (INSP) also detracted. This is an example of a company for which we reviewed the investment thesis and decided to move on. We sold the position during the quarter. Inspire develops implantable neurostimulators for treating obstructive sleep apnea. The company’s share price declined in August after management downgraded full-year 2025 guidance due to delays in the U.S. commercial rollout of its next- generation InspireV system. The delayed rollout of InspireV raised questions about execution by management. Additionally, concerns are growing about how GLP-1 drugs may affect Inspire’s addressable market.

Medpace Holdings, Inc.

Medpace Holdings, Inc. (MEDP) was the top contributor to Fund performance. The contract research organization provides drug development, clinical trial management, and regulatory services to biotech, pharmaceutical and medical-device companies. Medpace shares jumped after management reported strong revenue and earnings growth and raised full-year guidance. We believe the company’s results are particularly impressive in light of the fact that there’s been a slowdown in the biotech funding cycle.

Fabrinet

Another top contributor was Fabrinet (FN). The company designs, manufactures and tests complex optoelectronic and photonic components, modules, and precision optical assemblies for customers in a variety of industries. Some of Fabrinet’s products are used in data centers, which ties the company somewhat to artificial intelligence (AI). AI has been a popular theme among investors, and likely helped drive the stock higher. However, we don’t hold Fabrinet’s stock as a thematic play on AI. The company is experiencing strong adoption of its products by both new and existing customers. We believe it’s well positioned to grow regardless of AI.

Nova Ltd.

Nova Ltd. (NVMI) also contributed. Nova sells metrology and process control systems used in semiconductor manufacturing. Like Fabrinet, the firm benefits from the increasing use of AI. Nova’s ties to the AI theme likely helped lift the stock during the quarter. But here too, we aren’t investing in Nova as part of a thematic view, and we don’t view it as a speculative business. The company has a strong track record of earnings growth, in our view, and we believe its products will continue to be in high demand as semiconductor manufacturing becomes more complex. (Current and future holdings are subject to risk and change.)

Past performance does not guarantee future results. Information in this document regarding market or economic trends, or the factors influencing historical or future performance, reflects the opinions of management as of the date of this document. These statements should not be relied upon for any other purpose. Current performance may be lower or higher than the data quoted. To obtain the most recent month-end performance data available, please visit wasatchglobal. com. The Advisor may absorb certain Fund expenses, without which total returns would have been lower. Investment returns and principal value will fluctuate and shares, when redeemed, may be worth more or less than their original cost.Total Expense Ratio: Investor Class—Gross 1.75%, Net 1.01% / Institutional Class—Gross 1.26%, Net 0.86% Total Annual Fund Operating Expenses include operating expenses, including the management fee, before any expense reimbursements by the Advisor. The Advisor has contractually agreed to limit certain expenses to 1.00% for the Investor Class and 0.85% for the Institutional Class through at least 1/31/2026. See the prospectus for additional information regarding Fund expenses. Wasatch Funds will deduct a 2.00% redemption fee on Fund shares held 60 days or less. The performance data does not reflect the deduction of fees or taxes, which if reflected, would reduce the performance quoted.For more complete information including charges, risks and expenses, read the prospectus carefully. data.title: Wasatch U.S. Select Fund Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Overview

U.S. stocks continued to rally in the third quarter. The Federal Reserve’s interest rate cut helped buoy stocks, as did economic data showing that, while there may be some pockets of concern, the U.S. economy remained steady. In this environment, the Russell Midcap® Growth Index was up 2.78%. The Wasatch U.S. Select Fund—Investor Class did not keep pace and finished the period down -1.93%.

We owned a handful of stocks that fell during the period. Given the concentrated nature of the Fund, those holdings had a large effect on results. A few of the companies reported slower revenue growth and their stocks were down substantially. For most of those companies, investors seemed to be debating whether the slowdown was due to the macroeconomic environment or to competitive pressures.

We have worked through that same debate. One of the ways in which we monitor whether the growth potential and quality of a company is still intact is through our “critical questions.” For every company in which we invest, we create two or three critical questions designed to capture long-term insights about the business that if we knew the answer today would cause us to significantly change our position. These questions help us focus our research on where we think the company could be in five or 10 years.

For Fund holdings that detracted from quarterly performance, we reviewed our critical questions again. Although we sold a few stocks, we believe the long-term investment thesis remains intact for most of the holdings that detracted from performance.

Another reason the Fund underperformed is because, in much of our coverage universe, stocks of low-quality companies have been in favor. This is a direct headwind for our investment process, which seeks to identify high- quality, long-duration growth companies. We can’t predict when the period favoring low-quality companies will end. But periods of outperformance by low-quality stocks have historically been short-lived. The stocks of high-quality businesses have outperformed over the long term. We believe that will continue to be the case.

Details of The Quarter

BellRing Brands, Inc. (BRBR) was the largest detractor from Fund performance during the quarter. BellRing is a consumer-staples company known for its Premier Protein line and other convenient-nutrition products. What’s weighed on the stock most is that one of BellRing’s club channel partners is expanding the floor space of the protein shake category and giving some space to other competitors. We spoke with management after that news and reevaluated the business. We came out of that work with continued conviction in the company. We believe BellRing has maintained its leadership position, and that this floor space expansion reflects the health of the category. Looking ahead, we continue to believe BellRing has headroom to double its business, supported by category growth, sourcing share from incumbent brands, and remaining a leader in its space.

Another detractor was Shift4 Payments, Inc. (FOUR). A longtime holding in many of our small-cap funds, Shift4 provides payment processing solutions for hospitality, retail, and e‑commerce businesses. Fundamentals for the company remain solid, as organic revenues grew in the high-teens percentages in the most recent quarter. But the stock has been down in part due to concerns about how a softer macroeconomic environment could affect the company. Additionally, investor sentiment on the company became less favorable after its acquisition of Global Blue, which is a foray into a new customer segment for Shift4. The acquisition also means the company is taking on more debt. Acquisitions can sometimes cause investors angst since they take time to integrate into a company’s normal operations. However, we have confidence in Shift4’s leadership and their ability to execute on Global Blue.

Inspire Medical Systems, Inc. (INSP) also detracted. This is an example of a company for which we reviewed the investment thesis and decided to move on. We sold the position during the quarter. Inspire develops implantable neurostimulators for treating obstructive sleep apnea. The company’s share price declined in August after management downgraded full-year 2025 guidance due to delays in the U.S. commercial rollout of its next- generation InspireV system. The delayed rollout of InspireV raised questions about execution by management. Additionally, concerns are growing about how GLP-1 drugs may affect Inspire’s addressable market.

Medpace Holdings, Inc. (MEDP) was the top contributor to Fund performance. The contract research organization provides drug development, clinical trial management, and regulatory services to biotech, pharmaceutical and medical-device companies. Medpace shares jumped after management reported strong revenue and earnings growth and raised full-year guidance. We believe the company’s results are particularly impressive in light of the fact that there’s been a slowdown in the biotech funding cycle.

Another top contributor was Fabrinet (FN). The company designs, manufactures and tests complex optoelectronic and photonic components, modules, and precision optical assemblies for customers in a variety of industries. Some of Fabrinet’s products are used in data centers, which ties the company somewhat to artificial intelligence (AI). AI has been a popular theme among investors, and likely helped drive the stock higher. However, we don’t hold Fabrinet’s stock as a thematic play on AI. The company is experiencing strong adoption of its products by both new and existing customers. We believe it’s well positioned to grow regardless of AI.

Nova Ltd. (NVMI) also contributed. Nova sells metrology and process control systems used in semiconductor manufacturing. Like Fabrinet, the firm benefits from the increasing use of AI. Nova’s ties to the AI theme likely helped lift the stock during the quarter. But here too, we aren’t investing in Nova as part of a thematic view, and we don’t view it as a speculative business. The company has a strong track record of earnings growth, in our view, and we believe its products will continue to be in high demand as semiconductor manufacturing becomes more complex. (Current and future holdings are subject to risk and change.)

Outlook

While we were disappointed with the Fund’s performance this quarter, we aren’t deviating from our investment process. During Wasatch’s 50-year history, we've learned the importance of staying committed to our philosophy and process, as opposed to chasing what’s currently “working” in the market. We’ll always do our best to be open- minded about new opportunities that could unfold, but we’ll stay true to our discipline of finding high-quality, long- duration growth companies.

As we look across the Fund, we remain encouraged by the fundamentals of our companies, and by the high- quality nature of these businesses. Earnings growth for most companies held by the Fund remains strong. This makes us excited about the Fund’s return potential over a three- to five-year time horizon.

Thank you for the opportunity to manage your assets.

Sincerely,

Mike Valentine, Paul Lambert, Austin Bone and Mick Rasmussen

data.title: Thrivent Small Cap Growth Fund Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Executive Summary

  • The Fund underperformed the Russell 2000 Growth Index during the third quarter.
  • The Fund underperformed the Russell 2000 Growth Index during the last 12-months.
  • From a sector standpoint, the biggest changes were the following: Consumer Discretionary overweight increased, Financials underweight decreased, Industrials overweight decreased, Health Care underweight increased.
  • These changes are more a product of new idea timing rather than a tactical change.

Performance factors

Thrivent Small Cap Growth Fund underperformed the Russell 2000® Growth Index for the quarter. The underperformance was driven primarily by stock selection in the Health Care, Information Technology, and Industrials sectors.

In Health Care, CorVel Corporation (CRVL), a healthcare managed care and claims management organization, underperformed in the quarter as the company’s largest segment, patient management, experienced softer growth and decreasing margins. Inspire Medical Systems, Inc (INSP), a medical technology company that has developed and commercialized minimally invasive solutions for obstructive sleep apnea, saw growth slow amid a national rollout of their new InspireV product. In addition, margin expectations were lowered as the company intends to spend significantly more on marketing in the coming quarters. In Information Technology, a majority of the Fund’s underperformance came in the application software industry where several names the Fund owned underperformed and in the semiconductor technology industry, where several names the Fund did not own outperformed, causing a cumulative drag on performance. In Industrials, all the underperformance came from one company that the Fund did not own. This company was evaluated and did not meet the investment process at this time.

Partially offsetting these negatives were positive contributions from CECO Environmental Corp, an industrial technology company focused on delivering solutions for management of air quality, water treatment, and emissions, Modine Manufacturing Company (MOD), a thermal management company focused on manufacturing mission-critical solutions for data centers, and Installed Building Products Inc (IBP), a building products company serving the US residential industry.

For the last 12 months the Fund underperformed the Index with most of the underperformance due to poor stock selection in Health Care, Industrials, and Information Technology sectors.

The underperformance in Health Care was driven largely by stock selection. RxSight Inc (RXST), a medical technology company that pioneered the world’s first post-implant adjustable intraocular lens for cataract surgery, saw growth in utilization for procedures stall amid macro concerns and recent launches of competitive products. Inspire Medical Systems and CorVel Corporation contributed negatively, as discussed above. In Industrials, a majority of the underperformance in the sector came from two companies which the Fund did not own. Similarly, in Information Technology, all the sector underperformance came from two companies which the Fund did not own. In both these situations, the names were evaluated and did not meet the Fund’s investment process at this time. Additionally, Onto Innovation (ONTO), a manufacturer of process control solutions and inspection systems, has product leadership in several growth categories that are gaining momentum, but has failed to meet lofty expectations thus far for continued AI (artificial intelligence) demand and expansion.

Offsetting the negative performance in Health Care, Industrials, and Information Technology was positive stock selection in communication services and energy sectors.

While sector positioning of the Fund is a by-product of our stock selection process, relative sector weights are monitored and managed in the context of a rigorous approach to risk management. In the quarter, Health Care underweight was increased and Consumer Staples overweight was reduced as names were sold where the thesis played out or was wrong. The Consumer Discretionary and Information Technology overweight was increased as new names were added to the Fund.

Portfolio outlook

With the current level of business uncertainty and the Federal Funds rate remaining at elevated levels, it is unclear by how much and how long the economy will be impacted. However, investor sentiment about the economy fluctuates much more rapidly and with greater amplitude than actual changes in economic activity. These swings in sentiment often present opportunities. As a result, positions were added to or initiated in consumer discretionary and information technology, while other positions were reduced or sold as investment theses played out or proved no longer valid (health care and consumer staples). Our focus remains on finding quality growth companies with significant opportunities for growth in sales, income, and free cash flow.

Performance For the period ending September 30, 2025 • Periods less than one year are not annualized.

data.title: Citigroup: Turnaround Thesis Is As Strong As Ever
data.description:
Back in August, I stated that the turnaround for Citigroup, Inc. (C)(CITI:CA) was starting to gain momentum as both the top and bottom lines saw acceleration. With the P/B ratio below the sector median, I concluded that the risk/reward was attractive and reiterated my buy rating. The stock has since performed decently, as there have been gains of just over 5% since the publishing of that article. The bank reported their earnings just a few weeks back, and today, we will be conducting another analysis.

In the below discussion, further signs of a turnaround are shown. Revenue growth continued to accelerate with strength in all segments, and operating efficiency was able to lead to strong profitability. Guidance was barely changed for the full year, and so the outlook remains positive at this point. The P/B ratio has expanded very slightly since my previous article, but given the increasingly clear signs of a turnaround, I would say that the valuation still offers ample opportunity now. As a result, I am maintaining my buy rating on Citigroup.

Revenue Growth Accelerates

For 2025 Q3, Citigroup reported total revenues of $22.1 billion, and that is up 9% YoY. We will talk a bit more about their segments in just a second, but as you can see above, all of their five main segments saw good YoY growth in the quarter, and so there was widespread strength in their business. The company-wide top line growth rate is another acceleration as 8% of growth was reported in their Q2. Of course this is an encouraging sign and shows that the turnaround continues to progress in terms of business activity. Furthermore, they beat analyst expectations by a rather large margin of $907.32 million, and so Q3 was really a highly respectable quarter for the bank.

Segments Overview

Services

Firstly, CEO Jane Fraser stated that "Services posted its best quarter ever with revenues up 7%" and so it should be clear that the strength of this segment was significant. They reported robustness in Treasury & Trade Solutions and in Securities Services as growth was 7% in both units. A highlight was net interest income as there was an overall 11% increase YoY as they saw higher deposit balances and deposit spreads. With the Federal Reserve on a potential path to more rate cuts, investors should monitor to see if their spreads will be pressured in the coming quarters. Overall operating efficiency was high in this segment as expenses only increased by 5%.

Markets

Despite calmer markets over the summer than back in the spring months after Liberation Day, Citigroup was still able to post robust Markets results. Total revenues were up 15% YoY, and while both Fixed Income and Equity saw good growth, Equity led the way in terms of growth at 24%. In terms of Fixed Income, they saw particularly strong performance in their Rates and Currencies as client activity was high. As for Equity, derivatives saw robust activity as well. Like their Services segment, expenses were up just 5%, and so the bank's efficiency actions seem to be working out quite effectively.

Banking

Their Banking segment saw very encouraging results. Firstly, total revenues increased by 34%, but the breakdown of these revenues is where the good news is. Investment Banking revenues saw a 23% increase, and so it seems that dealmaking is really making a significant comeback for the company and perhaps for the entire sector. The government shutdown may weigh on Q4 investment banking results but overall, Q3 results are a good sign. Another good sign is the 49% revenue increase in Corporate Lending. Despite worries of a global economic slowdown, the strength in this unit shows that economic activity remains more than resilient at this point. Lastly, expense management in this segment was superb, as there was only a 2% increase YoY.

Wealth

For their Wealth segment, revenues were up by 8%, but there was some weakness in Wealth at Work. Nonetheless, net interest income was up by 8% YoY as there were higher deposit spreads as well. Like in their Services segment, it'll be interesting to see how these spreads hold up after 2 rate cuts and perhaps more rate cuts in the near future. Again, operating efficiency was strong as expenses increased by just 4% YoY.

U.S. Personal Banking

Lastly, while there have been significant worries about U.S. consumer confidence, there are signs in this segment that spending is still holding up. For U.S. Personal Banking as a whole, revenues increased by 7%. The largest contributor to revenues was Branded Cards with revenues up 8%, and so it seems that consumers are still spending despite an uncertain tariff environment. Expenses in this segment were flat, which is a good sign, and while there were some U.S. card credit losses, overall credit costs were down 4% YoY, so there shouldn't be too much concern here.

EPS & Returns

With top-line growth having accelerated from Q2 and expense control shown to be strong in each of their five segments, it can be assumed that their EPS and returns are strong. For EPS, they reported $1.86, up 23% YoY. That is actually a bit of a deceleration in growth compared to Q2's 29%, but nonetheless, with bottom line growth greatly exceeding the top-line growth rate, profitability was strong in the quarter. They also managed to beat expectations by a good margin of $0.14. In percentage terms, their bottom line beat even more than their top line, and so analysts were likely impressed by their expense management.

In terms of return ratios, there were significant improvements as well. ROE is up from 6.2% to 7.1% YoY, while ROTCE expanded from 7.0% to 8.0%. These are no small improvements, and so investors should be very happy with the profitability and capital efficiency trends they are seeing. Lastly, it is worth briefly mentioning here that they returned ~$6.1 billion to shareholders in the quarter, and their payout ratio is at 176%. That should be another cause of shareholder delight.

Full Year Outlook

I discussed Citigroup's full-year guidance in my previous article and concluded that the outlook remained positive. After their Q3, there were very minor changes, and so I will only briefly discuss them here. For revenues, they raised their guidance from around $84 billion to now higher than $84 billion, and so essentially they delivered a beat and raise for the top line. Net interest income excluding markets guidance was also raised. However, estimated expenses for the full year was also increased from around $53.4 billion to higher than $53.4 billion and so that might offset some of that revenue strength. Other than that, not much else has changed, and so overall I would say that 2025 continues to look like a solid year for the bank.

Valuation Still Attractive

In my previous article, the P/B ratio was at a reading of 0.896, and so there has been some modest expansion since then to 0.933. I believe this valuation continues to offer a good risk/reward to investors. In my August update, I found strong indications that the turnaround was gaining momentum, and in today's analysis, I found no different. Revenue growth was strong throughout their segments even as the macro environment remained shaky. Furthermore, as a result of the superb expense management, operating efficiency was high, and overall profitability of the business was rewarded. I would say Citigroup's Q3 results provide further evidence that the turnaround is happening, and so the very mild valuation expansion since my previous article is really not a deterrent for long term investors. Furthermore, Seeking Alpha shows that their P/B is still more than 20% below the Financials sector's median, and so I would say the stock is still quite cheaply valued considering the strengthening fundamentals.

Risks

I already mentioned two risks above. Spread pressure from potentially more rate cuts from the Fed as well as an investment banking slowdown in Q4 due to the government shutdown. I believe another potential risk would be if the Fed doesn't cut enough. Sure, more rate cuts may lead to lower spreads and lower profitability for Citi, but on the other hand, if the economy stalls due to the lack of additional rate cuts, the negative effects may be even worse. Recall from above that Corporate Lending was a key growth driver in their Q3. Well, if the economy slows, companies will have less incentive to borrow to fund their original growth strategy. With some Fed officials indicating the preference for holding rates as they are now, this is definitely something investors should have in mind.

Conclusion

Citigroup was on a path to recovery, and that continues to be the case. As stated just above, the fundamentals are improving on many measures, and so it seems that CEO Jane Fraser is doing a great job at improving key areas for the bank. Shareholders should also be cheering the strong ROE and generous capital returns, and so investors looking for income should be tempted. The valuation in the form of the P/B ratio has expanded slightly since August, but the deep discount to the sector shows that Citigroup remains very attractively priced. Therefore, a continued buy rating is justified in my view.
